name: Cuban Missile Crisis 1962
description: A crisis notebook showing how missile sites in Cuba, a naval quarantine line, and high-tempo decision-making converged in October 1962.

sides.name: Soviet and Cuban missile complex
sides.description: Soviet deployments in Cuba altered the strategic map, while Cuban air defense and state control shaped what the U.S. could observe and threaten.

subUnits.name: Soviet missile regiments in Cuba
subUnits.description:
The offensive missile deployment was the heart of the crisis and the reason every reconnaissance image mattered so much.
- Role: Strategic missile deployment
- Geographic anchor: Distributed across western and central Cuba, including the San Cristobal area and other launch complexes.
- Why it matters: The sites transformed Cuba from a political problem into a nuclear warning-time problem.
- What to watch: Construction status, missile readiness, and whether launch complexes remained under Soviet operational control.

subUnits.name: Cuban air defense network
subUnits.description:
Cuban and Soviet-controlled air defense systems shaped reconnaissance risk and the danger of accidental escalation.
- Role: Air defense and reconnaissance denial
- Geographic anchor: Spread across Cuba with particular relevance near sensitive missile areas and key western approach routes.
- Why it matters: Air defense mattered because the U.S. needed continuing intelligence, but each flight carried escalation risk.
- What to watch: Rules of engagement, radar coverage, and the danger created by repeated overflights.

sides.name: United States response
sides.description: American decision-making split across intelligence collection, naval quarantine operations, and broader strategic readiness.

subUnits.name: U.S. quarantine force
subUnits.description:
Naval power projected the U.S. response onto a visible line at sea, allowing coercion without immediate strikes on Cuba.
- Role: Naval quarantine and interception force
- Geographic anchor: Positioned north and east of Cuba to regulate the maritime approaches during the quarantine.
- Why it matters: The quarantine created a physical line where resolve could be shown while preserving space for diplomacy.
- What to watch: Boarding decisions, Soviet ship movements, and whether naval pressure remained proportional.

subUnits.name: ExComm and strategic readiness system
subUnits.description:
Civilian and military crisis managers had to interpret intelligence, signal resolve, and avoid a sequence of moves that closed off diplomacy.
- Role: Strategic command and crisis decision node
- Geographic anchor: Centered on Washington and the wider U.S. strategic posture, but operationally tied to reconnaissance and the maritime quarantine around Cuba.
- Why it matters: The crisis was governed as much from briefing rooms and command centers as from ships and launch complexes.
- What to watch: Escalation control, intelligence confidence, and the handling of simultaneous military and diplomatic channels.

events.title: U-2 imagery confirms missile deployment
events.description: Reconnaissance photography revealed Soviet missile sites in Cuba, giving the U.S. leadership a concrete map of the emerging strategic threat.

events.title: Kennedy announces the quarantine
events.description: The televised address translated intelligence and policy into a public geographic frame: offensive weapons in Cuba and a quarantine line designed to block further escalation.

events.title: The quarantine line goes active
events.description: With U.S. naval forces in position, the crisis moved from intelligence and speech into direct physical enforcement at sea.

events.title: Black Saturday pushes the crisis to the edge
events.description: The shootdown of a U-2 over Cuba and other simultaneous pressures made 27 October the most dangerous day of the crisis.

events.title: Khrushchev accepts a settlement
events.description: Moscow agreed to remove the missiles from Cuba, allowing the crisis to shift from immediate confrontation to verification and rollback.

events.title: The quarantine is lifted
events.description: With bombers and key offensive systems removed, the United States lifted the quarantine and the crisis moved into its post-mortem phase.

meta.name: San Cristobal missile area
meta.description: Western Cuba site associated with the first widely cited reconnaissance images of Soviet missile deployment.

meta.name: Central Cuba missile zone
meta.description: A second launch-zone reference point showing that the deployment was not isolated to a single site.

meta.name: Naval quarantine line
meta.description: An illustrative line representing where U.S. naval power met Soviet shipping and where the crisis became physically enforceable.

meta.name: Guantanamo Bay
meta.description: The U.S. base at Guantanamo was an important flank reference in any map of the crisis.

meta.name: Perceived strategic threat radius
meta.description: Not a literal targeting diagram, but a visual reminder that the crisis was driven by the perceived compression of warning time from Cuban-based missiles.
